literature_search.user: Find recent research papers on synaptic plasticity in the hippocampus.
literature_search.ai_response:
I'll search the literature for recent papers on synaptic plasticity in the hippocampus.## Recent papers on synaptic plasticity in the hippocampus

Below are recent, relevant research and review articles (2022–2026) returned by the literature search. I included a short summary extracted from each result and a link to the article. If you’d like, I can attempt to fetch the full text for any publicly available paper using the read-paper tool.

### 1. **Behavioral timescale synaptic plasticity: properties, elements and functions** — Nature Neuroscience (2026-02-20)
- Summary: Describes **Behavioral Timescale Synaptic Plasticity (BTSP)** in the hippocampus: a bidirectional form of plasticity induced by single dendritic plateau potentials, capable of one-trial place-cell formation and linking plasticity to ongoing behavior. Discusses mechanisms, functions, and implications for memory storage and generalization.
- Link: [View article](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41593-026-02214-2?error=cookies_not_supported&code=321f96a4-f299-4845-85a8-7bae88601a98)
- Image:
  ![Figure from Nature Neuroscience BTSP paper](https://media.springernature.com/m685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41593-026-02214-2/MediaObjects/41593_2026_2214_Fig1_HTML.png)

---

### 2. **Synaptic plasticity rules driving representational shifting in the hippocampus** — Nature Neuroscience (2025-03-20)
- Summary: Compares plasticity rules (BTSP vs Hebbian STDP) using computational models and experimental place-field data. Concludes BTSP better explains trial-by-trial place-field shifting in CA1 (and to a different extent CA3), proposing a dynamic BTSP framework for ongoing representational drift during learning.
- Link: [View article](http://www.npg.nature.com/articles/s41593-025-01894-6)
- Image:
  ![Figure from Nature Neuroscience representational-shifting paper](https://media.springernature.com/m685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41593-025-01894-6/MediaObjects/41593_2025_1894_Fig1_HTML.png)

---

### 3. **Synaptic basis of feature selectivity in hippocampal neurons** — Nature (2024-12-18)
- Summary: Uses all-optical tools to measure spine-level synaptic weight changes before and after induction of place fields in CA1 during navigation. Reports temporally asymmetric, bidirectional synaptic plasticity kernels and compartment-specific expression (basal vs oblique dendrites), linking synaptic plasticity to rapid place-field emergence and BTSP-like phenomena.
- Link: [View article (PDF)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-024-08325-9.pdf)
- Image:
  ![Figure from Nature synaptic basis paper](https://media.springernature.com/m685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41586-024-08325-9/MediaObjects/41586_2024_8325_Fig1_HTML.png)

---

### 4. **Frontiers | The Intriguing Contribution of Hippocampal Long-Term Depression to Spatial Learning and Long-Term Memory** — Frontiers in Behavioral Neuroscience (2022-04-25)
- Summary: Review arguing that **LTD** plays distinct roles (not merely counterbalance to LTP) in hippocampus-dependent learning: pruning synapses, restricting LTP spatially, aiding pattern separation, and retaining content details of spatial representations.
- Link: [View article](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnbeh.2022.806356/full)
- Image:
  ![Figure from Frontiers LTD review](https://images-provider.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=1200&f=png/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/806356/fnbeh-16-806356-HTML-r1/image_m/fnbeh-16-806356-g001.jpg)

---

### 5. **Target cell-specific plasticity rules of NMDA receptor-mediated synaptic transmission in the hippocampus** — Frontiers in Cellular Neuroscience (2023-04-05)
- Summary: Experimental study (patch clamp, 2-photon Ca imaging, immuno-EM) showing target-cell specific rules for NMDAR-mediated plasticity at MF synapses (MF→CA3 vs MF→mossy cell). Finds distinct calcium dynamics and mGluR subtype involvement determine NMDAR-LTP vs NMDAR-LTD.
- Link: [View article](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fncel.2023.1068472/full)
- Image:
  ![Figure from Frontiers NMDAR plasticity paper](https://images-provider.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=1200&f=png/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1068472/fncel-17-1068472-HTML/image_m/fncel-17-1068472-g001.jpg)

---

### 6. **Frontiers | Interplay of epilepsy and long-term potentiation: implications for memory** — Frontiers in Neuroscience (2025-01-10)
- Summary: Review discussing how dysregulation of LTP/LTD contributes to hyperexcitability in epilepsy and its memory implications. Covers signaling pathways (MAPK, mTOR, WNT/β-catenin), neuroinflammation, and calcium signaling intersections with plasticity.
- Link: [View article](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnins.2024.1451740/full)
- Image:
  ![Figure from Frontiers epilepsy & LTP review](https://images-provider.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=1200&f=png/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1451740/fnins-18-1451740-HTML/image_m/fnins-18-1451740-g001.jpg)

---

### 7. **Convergence and divergence of molecular mechanisms in Hebbian and homeostatic plasticity** — Frontiers in Synaptic Neuroscience (2026-02-09)
- Summary: Review comparing molecular mechanisms of Hebbian (LTP/LTD) and homeostatic plasticity (synaptic scaling), emphasizing AMPAR regulation, scaffolding, signaling, adhesion, and secreted factors. Identifies convergent and divergent mechanisms and key gaps for hippocampal plasticity research.
- Link: [View article](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/synaptic-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnsyn.2026.1761008/full)
- Image:
  ![Figure from Frontiers Hebbian/homeostatic review](https://images-provider.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=1200&f=png/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1761008/fnsyn-18-1761008-HTML/image_m/fnsyn-18-1761008-g001.jpg)

---

software_docs_entitysdk.expected_output:
## Overview

Below are examples showing how to retrieve (list) all CellMorphology entities using the Open Brain Platform Python SDK (entitysdk). I include:

- A minimal example that fetches a limited set (safe default).
- A full \u201cget all\u201d pattern that pages through results until all items are retrieved.
- Notes about pagination and total_items (required by EntityCore rules).

All examples assume you have an authenticated `client` instance from the entitysdk (see your project/docs for authentication). Replace any placeholder values (API keys, endpoints) per your environment.

---

## 1) Minimal / quick preview (limit results)

This fetches a limited number of morphologies (e.g., 10) \u2014 useful for quick checks.

```python
# Example: quick fetch (limit 10)
from entitysdk import Client
from entitysdk.entities import CellMorphology

# instantiate client (fill in auth details per your setup)
client = Client(api_key=\YOUR_API_KEY\")  # adapt to your auth method

# search for CellMorphology with a limit (safe preview)
morphs = client.search_entity(entity_type=CellMorphology, limit=10).all()

print(f\Retrieved {len(morphs)} morphologies (preview)\")
for m in morphs:
    print(m.id, m.name)
```

---

## 2) Recommended: Retrieve ALL morphologies with pagination (robust)

The EntityCore \u201cGET ALL\u201d may paginate. This pattern requests pages until all items are retrieved. It also prints the reported total_items so you can decide whether to continue if needed.

```python
from entitysdk import Client
from entitysdk.entities import CellMorphology

client = Client(api_key=\YOUR_API_KEY\")

# page_size should be <= service max (commonly 10); adapt if docs show different max
page_size = 10
page = 1
all_morphs = []

while True:
    result = client.search_entity(
        entity_type=CellMorphology,
        limit=page_size,
        offset=(page - 1) * page_size  # note: some clients use page/offset; adjust if client API differs
    ).page(page)  # if client provides a page method; otherwise use offset/limit pattern

    # If your client returns a wrapper with items and total_items:
    items = result.items if hasattr(result, \items\") else result  # adapt to the client response structure
    total_items = getattr(result,       otal_items\", None)

    # Collect items
    all_morphs.extend(items)

    # Display progress
    if total_items is not None:
        print(f\Fetched page {page}. collected {len(all_morphs)} of {total_items}\")

    # Stop when fewer items returned than page_size OR we've reached total_items
    if len(items) < page_size:
        break
    if total_items is not None and len(all_morphs) >= total_items:
        break

    page += 1

print(f\Total morphologies retrieved: {len(all_morphs)}\")
```

Notes:
- The entitysdk has multiple helpers; if it exposes a `.all()` or `.iterate()` convenience that automatically pages, prefer that. Example from docs: `client.search_entity(entity_type=CellMorphology).all()`.
- Always check the client response structure for fields like `items`, `total_items`, `limit`, `offset`, or built-in iterators.

---

## 3) Using the entitysdk convenience method shown in examples (single call)

The entitysdk examples show a convenient pattern using search_entity(...).all() to fetch results. If the SDK supports retrieving all with `.all()`, use that:

```python
from entitysdk import Client
from entitysdk.entities import CellMorphology

client = Client(api_key=\YOUR_API_KEY\")

# If the client supports .all() to fetch everything (with internal paging)
morphs = client.search_entity(entity_type=CellMorphology).all()

print(f\Total morphologies returned: {len(morphs)}\")
for m in morphs[:10]:  # show first 10
    print(m.id, m.name)
```

---

## 4) Filtering examples (by mtype or species)

You can pass query filters to narrow results (examples from docs):

- Find morphologies for a specific MType label:

```python
morphs_sr_pc = client.search_entity(
    entity_type=CellMorphology,
```

- **10 single cell simulations on Pro:** 5 credits
- **100-neuron circuit for 1 second on Free:** 5 credits (100 × 0.05)
- **100-neuron circuit for 1 second on Pro:** 2.5 credits (100 × 0.025)

morphology_studies.user: What are some recent studies on neuron morphology in the cerebrum?
morphology_studies.ai_response:
I'll search recent literature on neuron morphology in the cerebrum (cerebral cortex and related regions). I'll return several recent, relevant papers and short summaries.## Recent studies on neuron morphology in the cerebrum (selected)

Below are recent, high-impact papers that study neuronal morphology across cerebral cortex / prefrontal cortex / visual cortex and related areas. For each paper I give a short summary, species & scale, and a direct link. I included figures when the search returned an image URL.

---

### 1) Electrophysiological classification of human layer 2–3 pyramidal neurons reveals subtype-specific synaptic interactions — Nature Neuroscience (2025)
- **Summary:** Whole-cell patch recordings and morphological reconstructions of >1,400 human L2–3 pyramidal neurons and ~1,400 identified monosynaptic connections from temporal cortex. The study defines four electrophysiological subtypes of L2–3 pyramidal neurons, links these subtypes to morphological differences, and shows subtype-specific synaptic connectivity patterns in human cortex.
- **Species / scale:** *Homo sapiens*; large single-cell electrophysiology + morphology dataset, human temporal cortex.
- **Key takeaways:** Subtype-specific microcircuit architecture in human superficial cortex; morphology corroborates electrophysiological classification.
- **Link:** [Electrophysiological classification of human layer 2–3 pyramidal neurons reveals subtype-specific synaptic interactions (Nature Neuroscience)](http://www.nature.com/articles/s41593-025-02134-7)  
![Figure](https://media.springernature.com/m685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41593-025-02134-7/MediaObjects/41593_2025_2134_Fig1_HTML.png)

---

### 2) Pyramidal neurons proportionately alter cortical interneuron subtypes — Nature (2026)
- **Summary:** Developmental / cell-fate study showing that pyramidal neuron populations influence the abundance and subtype identity of cortical interneurons (SST and PVALB subtypes) via distinct mechanisms (programmed cell death modulation and fate switching). Highlights molecular signalling between pyramidal neurons and interneurons that shapes cortical interneuron composition.
- **Species / scale:** Mouse genetics / developmental cortex studies.
- **Key takeaways:** Pyramidal neurons actively shape interneuron subtype proportions—implications for how morphological and cell-type composition co-develop in the cerebral cortex.
- **Link:** [Pyramidal neurons proportionately alter cortical interneuron subtypes (Nature)](http://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-025-09996-8)  
![Figure](https://media.springernature.com/m685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41586-025-09996-8/MediaObjects/41586_2025_9996_Fig1_HTML.png)

---

### 3) Single-neuron analysis of dendrites and axons reveals the network organization in mouse prefrontal cortex — Nature Neuroscience (2023)
- **Summary:** Complete dendrite and axon reconstructions for ~2,000 neurons in mouse prefrontal cortex (PFC). Identifies 24 morphologically distinguishable dendritic subtypes among pyramidal projection neurons, characterizes axonal projection patterns, and links dendritic/axonal morphology to predicted connectivity and electrophysiological phenotypes.
- **Species / scale:** *Mus musculus*; nearly 2,000 full single-neuron reconstructions in PFC.
- **Key takeaways:** Rich morphological repertoire in PFC; joint dendrite–axon analysis helps predict intra-column, inter-hemispheric and inter-column connectivity.
- **Link:** [Single-neuron analysis of dendrites and axons reveals the network organization in mouse prefrontal cortex (Nature Neuroscience, 2023)](http://www.nature.com/articles/s41593-023-01339-y)  
![Figure](https://media.springernature.com/m685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41593-023-01339-y/MediaObjects/41593_2023_1339_Fig1_HTML.png)

---

### 4) An unsupervised map of excitatory neuron dendritic morphology in the mouse visual cortex — Nature Communications (2025)
- **Summary:** Data-driven graph / machine-learning analysis of >30,000 excitatory neuron reconstructions from MICrONS serial-section EM volumes (V1 and higher visual areas). The authors argue cortical excitatory neuron morphology is better represented as a low-dimensional continuum (axes of variation) rather than strictly discrete morphological types, with notable layer- and area-specific trends.
- **Species / scale:** *Mus musculus*; very large-scale EM reconstructions (tens of thousands of neurons).
- **Key takeaways:** Proposes continuous morphological landscape for excitatory neurons, with layer-specific trends (e.g., tuft size changes with depth) and area-specific differences in layer 4.
- **Link:** (PDF returned in search results) [An unsupervised map of excitatory neuron dendritic morphology in the mouse visual cortex (Nature Communications)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-58763-w)

---

## Short synthesis / trends across these studies
- Large-scale single-neuron reconstructions (light microscopy + EM) are now enabling cortical morphological atlases at the tens of thousands to whole-millimeter scale.
- Studies increasingly combine morphology with electrophysiology, connectivity, and molecular data to define neuron subtypes more robustly (multi-modal classification).
- There is a shift from rigid discrete morphological “types” toward describing continuous axes of morphological variation (especially for excitatory neurons).
- Human cortical datasets (patch-seq / slice physiology + reconstructions) are maturing and reveal subtype-specific circuitry that complements mouse work.

---

If you’d like:
- I can retrieve full-text PDFs for any of these papers (and extract figures/Methods) — tell me which paper(s) to read and I’ll fetch them.
- Or I can search for morphology studies focused on a specific cerebral subregion (e.g., motor cortex, temporal cortex), species, or neuron class (pyramidal vs interneuron). Which would you prefer?

software_docs_obione.expected_output:
## Single Cell Simulation Example (OBI-One)

Below is a step-by-step single-cell simulation example using the OBI-One Python API (openbraininstitute/obi-one). It shows how to:

- select an MEModel (morphology+electrical model),
- build and validate a simulation configuration (stimulus, recordings, timestamps),
- generate a grid-scan / single simulation,
- and run the simulation with BlueCelluLab (the usual backend used in OBI-One examples).

You will need obi_one installed and configured, plus credentials / db_client as required by your environment. Replace placeholder IDs (e.g., <MEMODEL-ID>) and file paths with real values.

### 1) Select an MEModel to simulate
```python
# Option A: use a known MEModel ID
entity_ID = \"<MEMODEL-ID>\  # <<< Replace with real MEModel UUID

# Option B: (interactive selection helper used in examples)
# memodel_ids = get_entities.get_entities(\memodel\", token, memodel_ids,
#                                         project_context=project_context,
#                                         multi_select=False,
#                                         default_scale=\small\")
# memodel_ids[0] would then be used below
```

### 2) Build the MEModel simulation configuration (form API)
```python
from pathlib import Path
import obi_one as obi

# === Parameters ===
sim_duration = 3000.0  # ms

# Create an empty config for MEModel-based simulation
sim_conf = obi.MEModelSimulationScanConfig.empty_config()

# Info block
info = obi.Info(
    campaign_name=\MEModel Simulation\",
    campaign_description=\Single-cell MEModel simulation with constant current stimulus\"
)
sim_conf.set(info, name=\info\")

# Regular timestamps (example: a single repetition covering simulation)
regular_timestamps = obi.RegularTimestamps(start_time=0.0, number_of_repetitions=1, interval=sim_duration)
sim_conf.add(regular_timestamps, name='RegularTimestamps')

# Somatic current clamp stimulus (Constant current)
stimulus = obi.ConstantCurrentClampSomaticStimulus(
    timestamps=regular_timestamps.ref,
    duration=2000.0,
    amplitude=0.5  # nA (example)
)
sim_conf.add(stimulus, name=\CurrentClampInput\")

# Record soma voltage
voltage_recording = obi.SomaVoltageRecording()
sim_conf.add(voltage_recording, name='VoltageRecording')

# Optionally limit the time window recorded
time_window_voltage_recording = obi.TimeWindowSomaVoltageRecording(start_time=0.0, end_time=2000.0)
sim_conf.add(time_window_voltage_recording, name='TimeWindowVoltageRecording')

# Initialization: point to the MEModel by ID and set simulation length
simulations_initialize = obi.MEModelSimulationScanConfig.Initialize(
    circuit=obi.MEModelFromID(id_str=entity_ID),
    simulation_length=sim_duration
)
sim_conf.set(simulations_initialize, name='initialize')

# Validate and get validated configuration object
validated_sim_conf = sim_conf.validated_config()
print(\Validated config:\", validated_sim_conf)
```

### 3) Wrap config into a GridScan / single simulation configuration
(OBI-One uses GridScan tasks to generate concrete config files; for a single point you still create a grid with a single cell)
```python
# Create a GridScan generation task for the validated form
grid_scan = obi.GridScanGenerationTask(
    form=validated_sim_conf,
    coordinate_directory_option=\ZERO_INDEX\",
    output_root='../../../obi-output/memodel_simulations/grid_scan'  # change to desired output
)

# (Optional) inspect multi-value parameters or coordinates
grid_scan.multiple_value_parameters(display=True)
grid_scan.coordinate_parameters(display=True)

# Execute generation (requires db_client configured)
grid_scan.execute(db_client=db_client)
```

### 4) Run the generated simulation with BlueCelluLab backend
```python
from obi_one.scientific.library.simulation_execution import run

# Path to simulation_config.json from generated single config
simulation_config_path = grid_scan.single_configs[0].coordinate_output_root / \simulation_config.json\"
print(\Simulation config path:\", simulation_config_path)

# Run the simulation (bluecellulab is typical)
run(
    simulation_config=simulation_config_path,
    simulatorluecellulab\",  # or 
eurodamus\"
    save_nwb=False  # optionally save NWB
)
```

### 5) Load and analyze results with bluepysnap
```python
import bluepysnap

snap_simulation = bluepysnap.Simulation(simulation_config_path)
spikes = snap_simulation.spikes

print(\spikes time_start, time_stop, dt:\", spikes.time_start, spikes.time_stop, spikes.dt)
print(\population names:\", spikes.population_names)

# Access soma voltage report
soma_report = snap_simulation.reports['SomaVoltage']
print(\SomaVoltage time_start, time_stop, dt:\", soma_report.time_start, soma_report.time_stop, soma_report.dt)

# Example: inspect the head of the spike report dataframe
filtered = spikes.filter(t_start=spikes.time_start, t_stop=spikes.time_stop)
print(filtered.report.head())
```

## Notes, tips and placeholders
- **Replace** <MEMODEL-ID> with the actual MEModel UUID from EntityCore.
- You need a configured db_client variable (OBI-One examples use a DB client for GridScan generation) \u2014 ensure you have credentials and environment set up as per your installation.
- If you prefer to pick an MEModel interactively or via EntityCore queries, use the platform's EntityCore helpers to get MEModel IDs (examples in the repository show interactive selection).
- The examples above are adapted from the OBI-One example notebooks:
  - examples/F_single_cell_simulations/entitysdk_memodel_simulation.ipynb
  - examples/F_single_cell_simulations/entitysdk_memodel_with_synapses_simulation.ipynb

If you want, I can:
- provide a minimal runnable script adjusted to your environment (if you give me the MEModel ID and where your db_client/config is stored),
- or show how to query EntityCore for available MEModels and pick one programmatically. Which would you like?
